Investment_Thesis: Aging owner in appreciating SW Tulsa corridor — motivated exit likely within 3 years.

Owner_Name_If_Known: Carolyn Sue Ashley (widow) + heirs; Ty Hogan (manager)
Distress_Evidence: Founder Roy Ashley died Nov 2017. Widow + 4 daughters operating with no succession plan. Adjacent Admiral Flea Market closed 2019 due to owner health/retirement, eroding corridor traffic. Estate-run operations are classic motivated-exit scenarios. (Source: Reynolds Funeral Homes obit; Route 66 News; Yelp Feb 2026)
Real_Estate_Upside: Route 66 alignment (E Admiral Place); near I-244/US-412; east Tulsa redevelopment pressure; 65k sqft footprint with rezoning/mixed-use potential
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 74115 — significant and growing Hispanic population; same trade area as El Bazaar with 100+ Hispanic vendors on the Garnett corridor
Investment_Thesis: Post-founder estate market on Route 66 corridor — heirs lack operational passion and a motivated exit is imminent.
Risks_Red_Flags: Family may resist external sale while still operating; verify lease vs. own status
Source: Yelp / Reynolds Funeral Homes obit / Route 66 News
Notes: Direct outreach to Sue Ashley or Ty Hogan recommended

Distress_Evidence: Single-owner LLC with no apparent succession plan. Leased (not owned) property — lease expiration terms unknown and potentially unfavorable. No social media activity cadence; D&B classifies under 'Other Miscellaneous Retailers' — lightly formalized operation. (Source: D&B; FindTheMarkets; Yelp Feb 2026)
Real_Estate_Upside: S Garnett Rd at US-64/Hwy 412 interchange; high new retail/residential development pressure; Supermercados Morelos anchor next door confirms land value
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 74129 — among highest-density Hispanic census tracts in Tulsa County. Explicitly serves Mexican/Central American vendors: piñatas, quinceañera goods, saddles, Spanish-language food vendors
Investment_Thesis: Aging sole-operator running Tulsa's densest Hispanic bazaar at a highway interchange — lease expiration creates a forced conversation.
Risks_Red_Flags: Leased not owned; must verify lease timeline with owner before approach
Notes: Direct outreach to Juvenal Saldivar; ask about retirement timeline and lease expiration date

Full_Address: 2233 N Prairie Creek Rd (relocated from 9334 E RL Thornton Fwy)
Owner_Name_If_Known: Elias Ramirez (manager on record, did not respond to press per KERA News Dec 2024)
Distress_Evidence: Dallas Board of Adjustments approved Home Depot takeover of original Thornton/Buckner site Oct 2024. Management told vendors to vacate by March 31, 2025. Mgmt statement: 'We tried to obtain an extension but we could not.' Vendor advocate Juanita Arevalo: 'It's going to be a financial hardship.' 20+ year operation rebuilding foot traffic at inferior new location. (Source: KERA News Dec 6 2024)
Real_Estate_Upside: New Prairie Creek Rd site in dense SE Dallas/Mesquite commercial corridor; original site validated by national retail (Home Depot) absorbing it
Hispanic_Population_Context: SE Dallas 75227/75228 is majority Hispanic; market serves small Latino-owned businesses as primary affordable retail destination per KERA reporting
Investment_Thesis: Forced-displaced 20-year bazaar with proven vendor base now fighting for survival at inferior new location — acquisition window before operator stabilizes or folds.
Risks_Red_Flags: Dallas is not Tulsa metro; operator may recover if new location builds traffic; verify current operator situation
Notes: Forced relocation distress — approach management about acquisition of operation/goodwill

Size_Booths_Acres: 151,505 sqft building / 305,364 sqft (7 acres) land / 230 tenants
Owner_Name_If_Known: Not publicly named; LoopNet listing broker contact required
Distress_Evidence: $20M LoopNet listing pulled from active advertising — sale fell through, price cut pending, or owner withdrew. Built 1953 (70-yr structure). Deferred maintenance. Delisted = motivated seller who has not found a buyer. (Source: LoopNet; hhb.shop; Yelp Feb 2026)
Real_Estate_Upside: 7 acres on Harry Hines Blvd adjacent to Dallas Medical District, I-35E, Love Field. Land value exceeds bazaar income value. Mixed-use/hospital development pressure. ~10.15% cap rate at $20M ask; $1.97M NOI
Hispanic_Population_Context: 75220/75229 — dense Hispanic population; 300+ shops including quinceañera dresses, western wear, authentic Mexican food; spine of Dallas's Mexican-American commercial corridor
Investment_Thesis: 7-acre highway-fronting land play in the heart of Dallas's Latino corridor — bazaar income validates the land; redevelopment exits the position.
Risks_Red_Flags: $20M ask requires significant capital; confirm listing status and broker contact; Dallas not Tulsa metro
Notes: Contact LoopNet listing broker directly; confirm if property is still available post-delisting

Distress_Evidence: Shut down 2020–Sep 2023 (3 years) for city code violations (electrical, planning, zoning). Owner filed lawsuit against City of El Paso during closure. Reopened Sep 30 2023 only after meeting compliance conditions. Preceded by 2019 fire. 3-year forced closure + costly legal battle + ongoing regulatory burden = financially/emotionally fatigued owner. (Source: KFOX14; CBS4 Local)
Real_Estate_Upside: 9 acres on US-62/180 (Alameda Ave) — primary east-west highway connecting to I-10. El Paso's Lower Valley is one of the fastest-growing US-Mexico border corridors. Nearshoring/border trade driving industrial and logistics development on this corridor.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 79907 (Lower Valley) is 95%+ Hispanic; Bronco Swap Meet operating since 1985 with 300+ vendors and 500+ dependent families — deeply embedded in the Hispanic economy
Investment_Thesis: Battle-worn owner of a 9-acre highway site who just survived 3 years of city enforcement hell — Sandoval is a motivated seller waiting for the right conversation.
Risks_Red_Flags: El Paso is well outside Tulsa metro; regulatory history requires due diligence on current compliance status; verify permit standing before approach
Notes: Direct outreach to Eddie Sandoval / Sandoval Capital Inc. — reference the reopening hardship to open the conversation

Size_Booths_Acres: 46 ac total / 8.8 ac market / ~26,000 sqft vendor space
Asking_Price: Not listed (broker OM distributed 2023 — no public ask)
Owner_Name_If_Known: Jim and Debbie Fitzgerald (22+ year operators)
Distress_Evidence: July 2022 fire destroyed most of the 26,000 sqft market (cause: undetermined). Vendors lost $80K–$100K each with no insurance reimbursement. Property marketed for sale via Marcus & Millichap / TAG Industrial (June 2023 OM titled 'DON-WES FLEA MARKET/DONNA REDEVELOPMENT'). Month-to-month lease for 25 years at only $21,667/mo — severely under-priced for 46 acres.
Real_Estate_Upside: 46 acres 2 min from I-2 (US-83) in the McAllen–Donna logistics/nearshoring corridor. TAG Industrial OM positions site for industrial redevelopment. Land at $21,667/mo on MTM lease = dramatically underpriced.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 78537 is 94.4% Hispanic; 84.8% Spanish-speaking at home. Deep pulga culture — flea markets are primary retail infrastructure in the RGV.
Investment_Thesis: Post-fire operators already tested the market via a major commercial broker. MTM lease on 46 acres of prime I-2 corridor land at $21,667/mo is dramatically underpriced. Acquire the lease/business, stabilize vendor occupancy, operate as Hispanic bazaar or hold for industrial land appreciation.
Risks_Red_Flags: Fire origin undetermined — arson risk / deferred electrical maintenance. MTM lease = no security without renegotiation. Vendor base was devastated; occupancy recovery timeline unclear.
Source: Marcus & Millichap / TAG Industrial OM / KRGV
Notes: Broker-marketed sale in 2023 is the strongest distress signal — approach Fitzgeralds directly referencing the OM

Size_Booths_Acres: Unknown acreage / 80+ stalls destroyed in Aug 2024 fire alone
Owner_Name_If_Known: Not publicly disclosed (refused all media contact)
Distress_Evidence: Aug 16 2024: fire destroys 80+ stalls (electrical circuit breaker failure; 3 injuries incl. 2 firefighters). Feb 7–10 2025: second fire destroys ~30 more stalls. Fire marshals discovered people living inside — illegal occupancy code violation. Owner refused media contact at both incidents. Two major fires in 6 months with active code investigations. Possible uninsurability.
Real_Estate_Upside: Airline Dr (TX-8 Beltway area) is a major North Houston commercial corridor. Distressed, fire-damaged property likely acquirable at significant discount to land value.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 77037 is 89–91% Hispanic — one of the most concentrated Hispanic zip codes in Houston. Deep pulga culture along the Airline Dr corridor.
Investment_Thesis: Two fires in 6 months with active code investigations, illegal occupants, and a silent owner signal an operator at crisis point. Airline Dr in a 90%+ Hispanic ZIP is the ideal profile — step in post-fire, clear compliance, reposition as a modern indoor marketplace.
Risks_Red_Flags: Fire damage requires rebuild capital. Illegal occupancy creates title/permit complications. Owner is non-communicative — could indicate existing liens/debts. Harris County may have issued cease-operations orders.
Notes: Approach through a local intermediary — direct cold outreach may be rejected given owner's media-avoidance pattern

Owner_Name_If_Known: Not publicly confirmed; Buzz Cook listed as manager in some records
Distress_Evidence: July 2020: owners announced closure with two-week notice citing declining traffic — over 300 vendors left in limbo (Spectrum News Austin, KUT Radio). 2009: three-alarm fire destroyed ~200 of 500 booths, vendors received no reimbursement. Recent TripAdvisor reviews describe muddy conditions, few vendors, deteriorating infrastructure. 40+ year operation with no visible capital investment.
Real_Estate_Upside: 130+ acres on US-290 E — major Austin arterial with explosive eastward commercial development pressure. Near Austin-Bergstrom International Airport. Land appreciation in this corridor is dramatic; 130 acres on a highway near a major airport is a significant underlying asset.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 78724 is 58–67% Hispanic. Located in East Austin, historically the primary Hispanic residential and commercial corridor in the city.
Investment_Thesis: A 40-year outdoor market that nearly closed in 2020, has a fire history, declining reviews, and sits on 130+ highway-adjacent acres in one of America's fastest-growing cities. Gap between distressed operating value and underlying land's redevelopment potential is enormous. Operate as Hispanic bazaar (serving 65% Hispanic ZIP) while holding land for appreciation.
Risks_Red_Flags: Owner identity unconfirmed — complicates approach. 130 acres may have drainage/environmental issues. Rising Austin land costs mean high property taxes. Possible ground lease rather than fee-simple ownership — verify title.
Source: KUT Radio / Spectrum News Austin / KXAN
Notes: Near-closure in 2020 is the clearest opening for a conversation — frame as rescue/transition, not hostile acquisition

Size_Booths_Acres: 77 acres total (market on northern portion; 40+ acres undeveloped)
Owner_Name_If_Known: Basilio and Josefa Gomez (founders, 1981); 9 adult children co-manage (Basilio Jr., Juani Parra, Adriana Condit, Alberto Gomez, Magda Camero, Luis Gomez, Mary Rosas, Claudia Gomez, Araceli Perez)
Distress_Evidence: Founded Easter 1981 as 'a sort of hobby' — aging founders with no formalized succession plan. Nine co-heirs managing without clear leadership structure creates fragmentation risk. March 2020: first closure in the market's 39-year history (COVID). 40+ years operating without developing undeveloped 77 acres — suggests capital scarcity or management capacity constraints.
Real_Estate_Upside: 77 acres on I-69E (formerly US-77) — major interstate corridor. Brownsville is one of TX's fastest-growing metros: SpaceX Boca Chica (20 mi away), border trade, nearshoring logistics boom. Undeveloped 40+ acres has significant industrial/commercial value. Near Brownsville/SPI International Airport and port.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 78526 is 92.4% Hispanic; 81.9% Spanish-speaking at home. Brownsville is one of the most Hispanic large cities in the U.S. Classic RGV pulga culture.
Investment_Thesis: Multi-generational family with aging founders and 9 co-heirs — textbook succession fragmentation. 77-acre property on I-69E in a SpaceX/border boom city is dramatically underutilized. Acquire business/land, operate modern Hispanic bazaar on existing footprint, hold undeveloped acreage in one of TX's hottest emerging markets.
Risks_Red_Flags: Nine-sibling ownership requires family consensus — complex negotiations. No active sale signal — speculative outreach. Market appears healthy; no acute distress. SpaceX-driven appreciation may push seller expectations above realistic acquisition price.
Notes: Approach through the founders directly (Basilio/Josefa) — frame as legacy preservation and retirement exit before the succession question forces a messy family negotiation
Contact_Info: facebook.com/77FleaMarket/ | Yelp listing active with phone

Owner_Name_If_Known: Tom Pyle, President — Corpus Christi Trade Center, Inc. (since founding 1982; confirmed via CorporationWiki and BBB)
Distress_Evidence: Operating since Jan 1, 1982 under the same single owner (44 years). KIII-TV reported vendors complained of sustained A/C failures in summer heat — vendors too afraid to speak on camera (fear of retaliation). A/C failure triggered by car crash to mechanical systems with no backup redundancy. Prior temporary closure covered by KIII ('set to reopen Friday'). Only 23 Yelp reviews after 44 years = severe engagement decline. No BBB accreditation.
Real_Estate_Upside: 100,000+ sqft climate-controlled building on S Padre Island Dr (SPID) — the primary commercial spine of Corpus Christi's south side. Corpus Christi growing as an energy/petrochemical hub and port city. Indoor format ideal for conversion to a modern Hispanic bazaar without major structural renovation.
Hispanic_Population_Context: ZIP 78415 is majority Hispanic; Corpus Christi overall is 64% Hispanic. Lower-middle income demographic (median HHI $53,803) — core value-oriented bazaar retail customer.
Investment_Thesis: Tom Pyle has run this 100K sqft indoor market since 1982 with no succession plan, documented vendor fear of retaliation, A/C failures in South Texas heat, and prior temporary closure. Ideal turnkey indoor building on the main commercial strip of a majority-Hispanic city — new operator takes over and repositions as a modern air-conditioned Hispanic bazaar.
Risks_Red_Flags: Building circa 1982 — potential capex for HVAC, electrical, structural. A/C complaints suggest deferred maintenance. No active sale signal. Corpus Christi is a smaller market (~320K population) vs. Houston or San Antonio.
Notes: Approach Tom Pyle directly — 44 years of ownership without a succession plan means the conversation is overdue; frame as legacy exit on his terms
